theme: raking a zen garden around rocks
title: Raked Garden Flowlines
mutation: composite: disjoint-path-cover output (vertex-disjoint rake lines with a coverage band) coupled to two objective terms derived from a single scalar potential -- distance-to-nearest-rock -- namely a weighted-uncovered penalty concentrated on the near-rock level sets (obstacle-level-sets) and the population variance of every sand cell's graph-gap to the nearest raked cell (spacing-variance-minimization); the winning strategy must design and threshold that potential rather than route lines one at a time.
strategies: trivial: fill sand cells in plain row-major reading order until the coverage floor is met (clusters at the top, ignores rocks and spacing entirely)

naive greedy: rule a fixed number of evenly spaced FULL rows straight across the whole garden -- excellent global spacing in open sand, but blind to where the rocks are, so it misses most of the near-rock ring zone whenever a rock's ring band happens to fall between two selected rows

insight (strong): compute the scalar potential D(cell) = multi-source BFS distance to the nearest rock (rocks that are close together automatically share a basin); unconditionally extract the sub-level-set {D <= M} first (guaranteeing the near-rock rings), then spend the remaining coverage budget on the same evenly-spaced sweep used by greedy -- so far-field spacing stays just as regular while the obstacle rings are never missed
a general local-search/ILP solver could go further: jointly optimize which level sets to keep and how to route the extra budget to also minimize spacing variance near basin boundaries/saddles
